name: Montecatini Pro
designer: Louise Fili, Andy Anzollitto, Nick Misani
classification_notes: Montecatini is a display typeface drawing on Italian Stile Liberty (Art Nouveau) travel poster lettering, with decorative ligatures, swashes and ornaments that place it firmly in the display/decorative category.
summary: A 24-style display family drawn from Italian Stile Liberty travel poster lettering of the early 1900s, with four widths, six weights and characteristic Art Nouveau ligatures.
description: Designed by Louise Fili, Andy Anzollitto and Nick Misani and released in 2017 with an expanded edition in 2019, the family spans four widths from Stretto to Largo across six weights from Light to Ultra. Distinctive ligatures are programmed as contextual alternates, pairing automatically as you type, and the font adds swashes, ornaments, stylistic sets and fraction support for full period-appropriate display work.
foundry_blurb: Tipofili describe Montecatini as drawing its cues from the elegant Stile Liberty travel posters of Italy in the early 1900s. The 24-style family spans 6 weights and 4 widths to create a vibrant typographic system. Typical of the Art Nouveau movement, Montecatini's distinctive ligatures provide flair while its widths and weights allow for dynamic copyfitting.

name: Portofino
classification_notes: Portofino is a display typeface with geometric underlying proportions and expressive, hand-lettered character, drawing directly from early twentieth-century Italian poster lettering rather than fitting neatly into any text classification.
summary: A display typeface by Louise Fili and Matthew Smith drawn from early twentieth-century Italian hand-lettered posters, with geometric proportions and an extensive suite of swashes and stylistic alternates.
description: Designed by Louise Fili and Matthew Smith and released by Tipofili in 2022, Portofino takes its character from the expressive quirkiness of Italian poster lettering of the early 1900s while keeping its underlying structure geometric. Six weights run from Thin to Bold, each accompanied by twelve stylistic sets, swash variants for a broad range of characters, ordinals, fractions and superscripts. The result is a typeface well suited to packaging, editorial headlines and branding where a distinctly Italian warmth is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Tipofili describe Portofino as a charming display typeface influenced by Italian hand-lettered posters from the early twentieth century, with expressive forms that complement its underlying geometric proportions. In keeping with the variety and quirkiness of the reference material, the family supports an extensive array of swashes and stylistic alternates across six weights.

name: Marseille
designer: Louise Fili, Andy Anzollitto, Nick Misani
classification_notes: Marseille is a display typeface drawing directly from Art Deco letterform traditions, with characteristic geometric elegance and adjustable crossbar heights that place it firmly outside workhorse text categories.
summary: A six-weight Art Deco display family derived from Louise Fili's cover lettering for Marguerite Duras's 'The Lover', with extensive OpenType features for crossbar and form variation.
description: Rooted in the lettering Louise Fili created for her iconic book cover design, Marseille brings a refined Art Deco sensibility across six weights from Thin to Bold. Eight stylistic sets allow fine control over crossbar heights, 'e' forms, alternate 'S', dotted 'I' and dieresis treatment, giving the typeface considerable expressive range from understated to ornate. Production was handled by Schriftlabor, with expanded language support added in 2018 covering over 300 Latin-script languages.
foundry_blurb: Tipofili describe Marseille as an Art Deco-inspired typeface based on Louise Fili's iconic cover design for The Lover, available in six irresistible weights. Adjustable crossbar heights and overshoots, combined with a rich set of OpenType features, allow the typeface to take on a variety of expressions while remaining timelessly elegant.
